scenario: I've been having really bad diarrhoea for the last 3 days
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "Uh no, it is, yes. Hello?"] Hi.
[Clinician asked: "Can you hear me OK? Great. Hello there. Um, before I go any further, can I confirm you name and your age please?"] Yes. So, my name is Robert.
[Clinician asked: "Robert?"] Yep.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Um, I'm forty eight.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Great."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "Excellent. And how can I help you this afternoon, sir?"] Right, so, uh, I've been having, uh, diarrhoea, for like, let's say past three days, more or less.

[Clinician asked: "OK, well I'm sorry to hear that. Uh can you tell me a bit more about your diarrhoea?"] Yeah, so it's been a, very bad diarrhoea, so it keeps me like, I keep going back and forth, to, to the toilet very often.
[Clinician asked: "Right."] Um, yeah it started like three days ago.
[Clinician asked: "Mm-hmm."] And uh, uh, so it started with vomiting, so I vomited a couple of times. Uh, but then, that went away, and, uh, yeah it's just diarrhoea now.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And you mentioned you've been um going to the toilet very often. How many times are you going a day?"] Yeah, around, five to six times a day.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And uh uh can you tell me, I know it's not nice to talk about, but is it quite watery, your stools, loose?"] Yes, it's definitely, definitely watery, uh, loose stools, yeah.
[Clinician asked: "OK. K. Any blood in your stools?"] No, not that I could see.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And uh uh uh with the diarrhea have you noticed any other symptoms, like tummy pain?"] Uh, not, not really, no. Uh, but I, I, I feel like, um, I can't, really drink too much, so I feel very dehydrated.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. When you say you can't drink too much, why is that?"] Uh, yeah because, really I, uh I, um, I'm like,  I feel like I don't drink enough, like I'm, I'm drink, I'm trying to drink, but then I just go to the loo, and I like, I, uh I, I, I do everything and I feel dehydrated again.
[Clinician asked: "OK, alright. Um have you managed to eat anything, solids?"] Uh,  not much, not much. So just a little bit, but I don't feel, um, I don't really feel like, uh, I have appetite.
[Clinician asked: "OK. You <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. OK. Um with your um, <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> the vomiting has now stopped. Is that right? You said"] Yes, yes.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK, alright. Have you had any other symptoms, like fever or temperature?"] Uh, so, not, really I , I feel a little bit warm, um, probably at, at the beginning I was feeling I had a bit of temperature, but now I feel, I feel kind of fine.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] I feel very weak though.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. Alright. Any headaches or muscle pains?"] Not, not really, no.

[Clinician asked: "OK, alright. And you mentioned three days ago. What were you doing when the symptoms started? Are you aware of any triggers?"] , not that I, I remember, what was I doing, I, I was at home I was working, on my computer and. Yeah, just felt, like some nausea and I had to, to vomit, and then I had some diarrhoea, straight after that.
[Clinician asked: "Yeah. Did you have any takeaway food, or any dodgy restaurant food?"] Uh, not that I remember, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Have you been around anyone unwell, with diarrhea recently, last week or so?"] Hmm, not that I know, not that I.
[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. Um, right. So moving on, um are you otherwise fit and well?"] Yeah, yeah, .

[Clinician asked: "Uh OK. And do you take any regular medications?"] Um, so I, I do suffer of asthma. So, I do have my inhalers, uh, that I you know, I need, I use infrequently, let's say only when I have, uh, a bit of .
[Clinician asked: "Is that, is that  inhaler?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Is that the blue inhaler? Blue inhaler. OK, alright. Any other medications that you take?"] Yes it is, yeah. Uh, no, nothing.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Do you have any allergies to medications?"] Um, not that I know of.

[Clinician asked: "OK. And any family history of any medical problems?"] Um, so, my father has, high uh, well used to have high blood pressure. He actually, so he actually died, of, bowel cancer, like ten years ago.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Sorry to hear that. Um, OK. And uh what about your mother?"] Ohh yes, so uh, probably is worth mentioning uh, she has diabetes.
[Clinician asked: "Diabetes. Do you know if that's type one or type two?"] Uh, it's type two.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Alright. Fine. And um, just a little bit more about your life in general, sir. Um who, who's at home with you? Who lives at home with you?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Who lives at home with you, sir?"] Ohh I, I live alone.
[Clinician asked: "You live alone, OK. Are you currently working at the moment?"] Uh, well I haven't been able to work in the past few days, but yes , like given, uh, my uh.
[Clinician asked: "What's your employment? What do you do for work?"] I'm a software engineer.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>. OK, alright. Have you taken time off work recently?"] Uh, yes, yes I have, uh. Uh, these last three days I've been feeling, really unwell, so I couldn't work.

[Clinician asked: "OK, alright. And just the last couple of questions with, regarding your smoking and alcohol. Do you do either of those?"] Uh, not really, I drink infrequently, like socially let's say, and I don't smoke.

[Clinician asked: "OK, alright. Um fine.  uh Robert um having listened to your story, I think you're probably suffering from what we call gastroenteritis, uh which essentially is just a, a tummy bug. It's an infection of your, your tummy. Um and normally lasts about three to five days, and gets better."] OK. All right.
[Clinician asked: "the things you can do to really help yourself get better soon, um are you can drink plenty of fluids. Really try and flush the toxins out of your body."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Uh you can get things from the pharmacy, which is called Dioralyte, which can help replenish any  uh minerals and vitamins you may have lost. Uh, it's called  Dioralyte. Um it's, it's a, it's a type of fluid replacement drink."] I'm sorry, what's the name of that? <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>
[Clinician asked: "Um, you can take some Paracetamol if you're feeling feverish and weak."] All right.

[Clinician asked: "I would probably advise you to continue taking time off work for next day or two, until you get better. normally you have to be diarrhea-free for about twenty four to forty eight hours, before you return back to work."] OK.

[Clinician asked: "Um, let's see how the next few  days go. And if you're still not better next week, I want you to come back and see me. And we can have another look at your symptoms, OK?"] That sounds good, that sounds good. Uh, thanks so much.

[Clinician asked: "Thanks.  do you have any questions for me, or?"] Um, no I think that's fine. Uh, yeah I'll, I'll try to , and drink , drink plenty of fluid and I hope it go away. Uh, if it doesn't I'll uh, I'll call, I'll call back. Is that OK?
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> I really hope you get better soon."] Thanks a lot. Thanks so much, for your time.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Bye bye."] OK. Bye.
gold_note:
3/7 hx ago developed vomiting. Has now developed diarrhea - 5-6x/day. Watery/loose stools. No blood in stools. No abdo pain. 
Reduced oral intake. Not drinking sufficient fluids. Feels dehydrated. Appetite reduced. 
No fever/temp, but feels warm. Feels weak. No headaches/muscle pain. 
Unaware of triggers. No unwell contacts 

PMH: asthma 
DH: salbutamol PRN 
NKDA
FH: father - HTN, died of bowel Ca. mother - diabetes Type 2
SH: lives alone , software engineer. Nil smoking, social EtOH

Imp: gastroenteritis 

Plan:
1.	Supportive mx - rest, fluids
2.	OTC diarolyte 
3.	Regular paracetamol
4.	Review in 3-5d INB, or earlier if worsening sx (diarrhea, vomiting, abdo pain, reduced oral intake)

gold_sections.PRESENTING_COMPLAINT: I've been having really bad diarrhoea for the last 3 days

naive.response_text:
Hello Robert,

Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. I understand how uncomfortable and exhausting dealing with diarrhea and vomiting can be, especially over several days. It’s good that the vomiting has stopped, but I know the diarrhea is still taking a toll on you.

Based on what you’ve described, it sounds like you’re experiencing gastroenteritis, which is a common infection that can cause these symptoms. The good news is that, in most cases, it clears up on its own within a few days with proper care.

To help you feel better, here are a few steps I recommend:

1. **Hydration is key**: Drink plenty of fluids to stay hydrated. Water, oral rehydration solutions like Dioralyte, and clear broths can be helpful. Avoid caffeine and alcohol for now.

2. **Electrolytes**: If you can, take Dioralyte or similar solutions to replenish any lost minerals and nutrients.

3. **Medication**: If you’re feeling feverish or uncomfortable, you can take Paracetamol to help with symptoms. Avoid other medications unless advised.

4. **Rest**: Make sure to get plenty of rest to allow your body to recover.

5. **Diet**: Once you start feeling better, gradually introduce bland foods like bananas, rice, applesauce, and toast. Avoid spicy or heavy foods until you’re fully recovered.

6. **Monitor symptoms**: Keep an eye on your symptoms. If the diarrhea persists beyond five days, if you develop a high fever, or if you notice blood in your stool, please come back for a follow-up.

Take things slowly and give yourself time to heal. If you have any questions or concerns in the meantime, don’t hesitate to reach out. I’m here to help.

Wishing you a speedy recovery,

[Your Name]  
[Your Title]

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, Robert. It sounds like you're feeling quite unwell with this diarrhea and vomiting, and I can understand how unsettling that must be. Having symptoms for three days, especially when they're so disruptive, is tough, and I want to make sure we address this together.

From what you've described, it seems like you might have a gastrointestinal infection, which is often caused by a virus or bacteria. These infections can lead to symptoms like diarrhea and vomiting, and they usually run their course within a few days. However, it's important to stay hydrated, as you've been experiencing, since dehydration can make you feel even worse.

To help you recover, I recommend continuing to drink plenty of fluids and using oral rehydration solutions like Dioralyte, which can help replace lost minerals and nutrients [PMID ?]. Over-the-counter medications like loperamide might help reduce the frequency of your diarrhea, but they should be used cautiously and only after discussing with a pharmacist or me [PMID ?]. If you're feeling feverish, you can take paracetamol to help with any discomfort.

It's crucial to rest and avoid work for the next few days until you're feeling better. Typically, you should be symptom-free for about 24-48 hours before returning to your usual activities. If your symptoms don't improve within a week, or if they worsen, please come back for a follow-up visit.

Before you leave, could you summarize the plan we've discussed? This will help ensure you understand the steps to take. We'll schedule a follow-up in one week to check on your progress. Please don't hesitate to reach out earlier if you need further assistance. I hope you feel better soon.
